serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Garlic Beef Cube, often served as a main dish with rice, is approximately 180 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with ginger and spring onion – HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo entry 'Beef, stir-fried with ginger and spring onion' as the closest comparable Chinese stir-fried beef dish available in the approved primary regional database. No direct entry for 'Garlic Beef Cube' exists in Foodo; the analogous entry shares the same primary ingredient (beef), cooking method (stir-frying), and sauce profile (soy-based).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) – Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Garlic Beef Cube'. GI estimated at 35 (low) by analogy with low-carbohydrate beef-based mixed dishes documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. The dish derives the majority of its energy from protein and fat; carbohydrate content is approximately 3.9 g per 100 g, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 2.46 per 180 g serving, consistent with low-GI classification (GI ≤ 55). Cornstarch and sugar contribute minor glycemic load. GI of 35 is consistent with similar stir-fried meat dishes in the published tables.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation due to its sodium content of approximately 370 mg per 100 g, and should consider requesting reduced-sodium preparations or limiting portion size.

type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content (approximately 3.9 g per 100 g) and low glycemic load, but portion control is still advised, especially when served with high-GI accompaniments such as white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat content (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g) in this dish and opt for leaner beef cuts or smaller portions to manage dietary saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Beef Cube is a popular Chinese stir-fry dish featuring tender, bite-sized pieces of beef, typically marinated and then quickly stir-fried with generous amounts of aromatic garlic. The beef is cubed to maximise surface area for flavour absorption and to ensure even cooking. The dish is characterised by a savory, slightly sweet, and umami-rich sauce commonly made with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and cornstarch for thickening. The texture is succulent and tender, with a pungent, deeply savoury garlic flavour. Nutritionally, it is a good source of protein (approximately 15 g per 100 g) and is relatively low in carbohydrates (approximately 3.9 g per 100 g), making it a satisfying main course with a low glycemic load. However, it is moderate in sodium (approximately 370 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g), which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is commonly served as a main course alongside steamed white rice in Chinese restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How to make garlic beef cube?
Nutritional value of garlic beef cube

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is adapted from the closest comparable entry in the HPB Singapore Foodo database ('Beef, stir-fried with ginger and spring onion'). Glycemic index is an analogous estimate anchored to Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed GI tables. Multilingual names and ontology codes are derived through research and closest-parent matches. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact entry for 'Garlic Beef Cube' exists in any approved primary source.
